nativeRange.habitat: Tropical coral reefs; characteristically chips out cavities in the centre of live, branching corals (e.g. Acropora, Pocillopora) and uses these as burrows. Reported across the central Indo-Pacific into southern China and northern Australia.

decorPreferences: deep live-rock rubble or branching coral skeletons that allow a self-chosen burrow
rockwork securely stacked so the shrimp cannot collapse it while excavating
acrylic tank strongly preferred over thin glass — smasher dactyl strikes have been reported to crack aquarium glass
tightly-fitting lid (smasher mantis are known to climb and jump when stressed)
sand/rubble substrate for digging and burrow extension

diet.notes: Strict carnivore and a smasher-type predator: kills hard-shelled prey (snails, hermit crabs, small crabs, bivalves) with high-velocity dactyl strikes. In captivity readily accepts frozen mysis, krill, chopped clam, shrimp, scallop, and squid; many individuals can be trained to take pellets and sinking carnivore foods. Feed every two to three days as adults; juveniles can be fed more often. Offering occasional live snails or hermit crabs is commonly advised to give the dactyl club natural wear and exercise.

goodWith: kept species-only — no permanent tankmates recommended
avoidWith: all snails, hermit crabs, ornamental shrimp, and small crabs (treated as food)
small fish (gobies, blennies, cardinalfish — readily taken)
other mantis shrimp (conspecific aggression is severe)
delicate live corals in the immediate burrow zone (will chip cavities into branching SPS)
any system recently treated with copper-based medications

summary: Compact (~7–12 cm) Indo-Pacific smasher mantis shrimp prized for its iridescent green body, red inter-segmental stripes, and bright orange meral spot. Same general care as the peacock mantis but with a noticeably smaller adult footprint and tank-volume requirement.
careNotes: A genuine smasher stomatopod with a calcified dactyl club capable of striking with enough force to crack thin aquarium glass — an acrylic tank or thick low-iron glass is widely recommended. Best treated as a single-occupant display in a small-to-medium species-only system: it will systematically hunt and kill snails, hermit crabs, ornamental shrimp, and small fish, and is highly territorial toward conspecifics. Provide deep live rock or coral rubble that lets it excavate or occupy a fixed burrow; in nature this species chips cavities directly into branching corals, so any live SPS placed close to the burrow is at risk. Generally regarded as intermediate in care because of its sensitivity to water quality (the Orange Spot is anecdotally more parameter-sensitive than the Peacock Mantis — cycling the tank for ~6 weeks before introduction is commonly advised) and the handler-safety issue rather than feeding difficulty. Like all marine crustaceans it is highly intolerant of copper-based medications. Handle with great caution: dactyl strikes have been known to draw blood and damage fingers; siphon tubes, magnetic algae scrapers, and acrylic shields are advisable when working in the tank. Source ranges for adult size varied (Blue Zoo Aquatics ~12.7 cm / 5 in; Lizard Island Field Guide 0.8–12 cm; aquarium-trade reports cluster at ~7–10 cm), so size is written as 7–12 cm. Tank minimums in the trade range from 10–24 US gallons (~38–90 L); written here as 100 L (~26 gal) to bias toward the larger value given the species' burrowing, glass-strike, and water-quality sensitivity. Reef2Reef Wiki (designated secondary) returned HTTP 403 and was swapped for Blue Zoo Aquatics, That Fish Place, Lizard Island Field Guide, and ReefCasa.
breedingNotes: Not regularly bred in home aquariums. All mantis shrimp in the trade are essentially wild-caught. In the wild, female stomatopods carry egg masses inside the burrow and brood them until hatching; larvae are long-lived plankton and require dedicated planktonic-rearing systems to survive. Captive rearing of Gonodactylaceus species is not documented in any of the hobby sources reviewed.

primary.name: Aqua Imports
primary.notes: Stated origin (Indo-Pacific) and target water parameters (74–78°F, pH 8.2–8.4, 35 ppt ≈ SG 1.026, NO3 <20 ppm). Product description was otherwise marked 'coming soon', so husbandry detail came from secondaries.

additional.name: Blue Zoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Designated Reef2Reef Wiki secondary returned HTTP 403; swapped to Blue Zoo Aquatics. Confirmed adult size ~5 in (~12.7 cm), minimum tank 24 gal (~90 L), Indo-Pacific origin, aggressive temperament, carnivorous diet (live food and frozen acceptable), reef-incompatible, species-only setup recommended, and acrylic-tank advisory (smashers can crack glass).

additional.name: Lizard Island Field Guide (Australian Museum)
additional.notes: Taxonomic authority: family Gonodactylidae, order Stomatopoda. Wild size 0.8–12 cm (largest goniodactylid). Documented behaviour of chipping cavities into branching coral. Coloration: dark green with red inter-segmental stripes plus the diagnostic bright orange meral spot; sexually dimorphic antennal scales (blue in males, orange/yellow in females).

additional.name: ReefCasa — The Ultimate Mantis Shrimp Pet Guide
additional.notes: Genus-level husbandry: target SG ~1.025, feed every ~3 days, captive lifespan up to ~10 years, generally hardy once parameters are stable, and all hobby specimens are wild-caught (no captive breeding established).

additional.name: Wikipedia — Gonodactylaceus
additional.notes: Used only to corroborate taxonomic placement (Gonodactylidae, Stomatopoda) and species authorship (de Man, 1902). Not used for husbandry parameters.

crustacean.moltingNotes: Adults are generally reported to molt every one to two months; juveniles molt more frequently. Stable salinity, calcium, magnesium, and iodine support successful molts. The shrimp typically retreats deep into its burrow before and after a molt and is particularly vulnerable for a few days while the new dactyl club hardens — disturbance should be minimised during this window.
crustacean.breedingNotes: Captive breeding of Gonodactylaceus ternatensis is not documented in the hobby sources reviewed; all trade specimens are wild-caught. Females brood eggs inside the burrow in the wild, and larvae are planktonic — rearing would require a dedicated planktonic system.
